prompt: Pharmaceutical product photography, vertical 4:5 portrait composition, of a clear borosilicate glass vial — 3ml capacity, slim cylindrical body with a narrow neck, exact proportions and silhouette matching the reference image. The vial is sealed with a gray rubber injection stopper crimped under a NAVY BLUE aluminum collar cap, topped with a matching NAVY BLUE plastic flip-off button. The cap is the only colored element — the rubber stopper underneath is medical gray. The vial sits centered on a pure clean white seamless studio background with soft even overhead lighting, a subtle natural soft shadow beneath the base, no harsh reflections, neutral white balance. A clean matte-white pharmaceutical label is wrapped around the lower two-thirds of the glass body, hugging the cylinder so it shows a slight curvature on the edges. Label content from top to bottom: the upper third of the label is completely blank pristine smooth white surface — absolutely no graphics, no text, no boxes, no dashed or dotted lines, no marker shapes, no logo placeholder symbols of any kind, just plain empty white. Centered below that empty area, the product name 'BPC-157' is rendered in clean modern medical sans-serif typography, in dark charcoal grey, crisp and sharp. Nothing else appears on the label — no other text, no icons, no decoration. Style: clinical, minimalist, premium pharmaceutical, modern medical packaging, commercial product shot, sharp focus, high resolution.
